title: Mock Interview: Lifecycle & Coroutines
description: Simulated L6 Android interview covering lifecycle awareness, coroutine mastery, and architecture design
lesson:
Interview Context

You're interviewing for L6 on the Gemini Enterprise Android team. This round focuses on Android lifecycle awareness and coroutine mastery. You'll be given code to fix, patterns to implement, and architecture to design. The interviewer expects you to identify subtle bugs, reason about threading and cancellation, and design production-quality async solutions.

Problem 1: Fix the Buggy ViewModel

The candidate receives this code for a chat streaming feature and is asked: "Find ALL the bugs."

// ViewModel class ChatViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _messages = MutableLiveData<List<Message>>() val messages: LiveData<List<Message>> = _messages fun streamResponse(prompt: String) { GlobalScope.launch { val response = api.streamChat(prompt) response.collect { chunk -> val current = _messages.value ?: emptyList() _messages.value = current + Message(chunk.text) } } } } // In Fragment: override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { lifecycleScope.launch { viewModel.messages.asFlow().collect { messages -> adapter.submitList(messages) } } viewModel.streamResponse(args.prompt) }

Bug Analysis (5 bugs):

Bug 1: GlobalScope — no lifecycle, leaks.
GlobalScope is never cancelled. If the user leaves the screen, the coroutine keeps running, holding references to the ViewModel and API response. This is a coroutine leak. Fix: Use viewModelScope.launch.
Bug 2: LiveData.value set from background thread — crash.
_messages.value = ... is called from whatever thread GlobalScope.launch runs on (Dispatchers.Default by default). LiveData.setValue() MUST be called from the main thread or it throws IllegalStateException. Fix: Use _messages.postValue() or switch to withContext(Dispatchers.Main).
Bug 3: No cancellation of previous stream on new request.
If streamResponse() is called twice (user sends another prompt), both streams run simultaneously and interleave messages into the same list. Fix: Track the Job and cancel the previous one, or use a MutableSharedFlow with flatMapLatest.
Bug 4: Flow collection not lifecycle-aware — wastes resources.
lifecycleScope.launch { ... collect } keeps collecting even when the Fragment is in the background (STOPPED state). It should only collect when the UI is visible. Fix: Use repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED).
Bug 5: streamResponse called every onViewCreated — double load on rotation.
onViewCreated is called again after configuration change (rotation). This re-triggers the API call even though the ViewModel already has data. Fix: Guard with a flag in the ViewModel, or trigger from init {}, or use SavedStateHandle to track if already loaded.

Corrected Code:

class ChatViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _messages = MutableStateFlow<List<Message>>(emptyList()) val messages: StateFlow<List<Message>> = _messages.asStateFlow() private var streamJob: Job? = null fun streamResponse(prompt: String) { streamJob?.cancel() // Cancel previous stream streamJob = viewModelScope.launch { // Lifecycle-bound val response = api.streamChat(prompt) response.collect { chunk -> _messages.update { current -> current + Message(chunk.text) // Thread-safe } } } } } // In Fragment: override fun onViewCreated(view: View, savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { viewLifecycleOwner.lifecycleScope.launch { viewLifecycleOwner.repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED) { viewModel.messages.collect { messages -> adapter.submitList(messages) } } } // Don't re-trigger on rotation — ViewModel already has state if (savedInstanceState == null) { viewModel.streamResponse(args.prompt) } }

Problem 2: Implement Retry with Exponential Backoff

The interviewer asks: "Implement a retryWithBackoff suspend function that retries up to N times with exponential delay (1s, 2s, 4s...), reports attempt progress via callback, and properly handles CancellationException."

Key Insight: CancellationException

NEVER catch CancellationException. Catching it breaks structured concurrency — the coroutine won't stop when its scope is cancelled. The coroutine framework throws CancellationException at suspension points (like delay()) to signal cancellation. If you swallow it, the coroutine becomes a zombie.

Why delay() Is the Cancellation Point

delay() is a suspend function that checks for cancellation. When the scope is cancelled, delay() throws CancellationException, unwinding the coroutine. If you used Thread.sleep() instead, the coroutine could never be cancelled during the wait.

suspend fun <T> retryWithBackoff( maxRetries: Int = 3, initialDelayMs: Long = 1000, onAttempt: (attempt: Int, maxAttempts: Int) -> Unit = { _, _ -> }, block: suspend () -> T ): T { var currentDelay = initialDelayMs repeat(maxRetries - 1) { attempt -> onAttempt(attempt + 1, maxRetries) try { return block() } catch (e: CancellationException) { throw e // NEVER swallow cancellation! } catch (e: Exception) { delay(currentDelay) // Cancellation-aware wait currentDelay *= 2 } } onAttempt(maxRetries, maxRetries) return block() // Final attempt — let it throw }

Full ViewModel Using It with StateFlow Progress

class ApiViewModel : ViewModel() { sealed class UiState { object Idle : UiState() data class Loading(val attempt: Int, val maxAttempts: Int) : UiState() data class Success(val data: String) : UiState() data class Error(val message: String) : UiState() } private val _uiState = MutableStateFlow<UiState>(UiState.Idle) val uiState: StateFlow<UiState> = _uiState.asStateFlow() fun fetchData() { viewModelScope.launch { _uiState.value = UiState.Loading(1, 3) try { val result = retryWithBackoff( maxRetries = 3, initialDelayMs = 1000, onAttempt = { attempt, max -> _uiState.value = UiState.Loading(attempt, max) } ) { api.getData() // May throw IOException } _uiState.value = UiState.Success(result) } catch (e: Exception) { _uiState.value = UiState.Error(e.message ?: "Unknown error") } } } }

Testing with TestDispatcher (delay skipped instantly)

@Test fun `retry succeeds on third attempt`() = runTest { var callCount = 0 val result = retryWithBackoff( maxRetries = 3, initialDelayMs = 1000 ) { callCount++ if (callCount < 3) throw IOException("Fail") "success" } assertEquals("success", result) assertEquals(3, callCount) // delay(1000) + delay(2000) = 3000ms virtual time // But runTest skips delays — test completes instantly! } @Test fun `retry respects cancellation`() = runTest { val job = launch { retryWithBackoff(maxRetries = 5, initialDelayMs = 10_000) { throw IOException("Always fails") } } advanceTimeBy(5000) job.cancel() // Cancel during a delay assertTrue(job.isCancelled) }

Problem 3: Configuration Change Survival

The interviewer shows this code and asks: "What survives rotation? What's lost? What survives process death?"

class NoteViewModel( private val savedStateHandle: SavedStateHandle ) : ViewModel() { var draftText = "" // A val notes = MutableStateFlow(emptyList<Note>()) // B val savedId: String? = savedStateHandle["noteId"] // C } @Composable fun NoteScreen(vm: NoteViewModel) { var localDraft by remember { mutableStateOf("") } // D var savedDraft by rememberSaveable { mutableStateOf("") } // E val notes by vm.notes.collectAsState() // F }

Complete Analysis:

VariableStorageRotationProcess DeathWhy
A draftTextViewModel field✅ Survives❌ LostViewModel outlives config changes but not process death
B notesViewModel StateFlow✅ Survives❌ LostSame as A — ViewModel memory only
C savedIdSavedStateHandle✅ Survives✅ SurvivesSavedStateHandle writes to Bundle, restored by framework
D localDraftremember { }❌ LOST❌ Lostremember is Composition-scoped — destroyed on recomposition from scratch
E savedDraftrememberSaveable { }✅ Survives✅ SurvivesrememberSaveable uses the saved instance state Bundle
F notes (collected)Backed by ViewModel StateFlow✅ Survives❌ LostcollectAsState resubscribes; StateFlow in ViewModel still has data
Survival Hierarchy: remember { } → Composition only (lost on rotation) │ ViewModel fields → Survives rotation (lost on process death) │ rememberSaveable { } → Survives rotation + process death (Bundle, max ~512KB) SavedStateHandle → Survives rotation + process death (Bundle, max ~512KB) │ Room / DataStore → Survives everything (persistent storage)

Problem 4: Design a Chunked File Download with Progress

The interviewer asks: "Design the coroutine architecture for: download a large file in chunks, process each chunk, show progress to the user, and allow cancellation at any point."

Architecture Solution:

class DownloadViewModel : ViewModel() { sealed class DownloadState { object Idle : DownloadState() data class Downloading(val progress: Float) : DownloadState() data class Processing(val progress: Float) : DownloadState() object Complete : DownloadState() data class Error(val message: String) : DownloadState() } private val _state = MutableStateFlow<DownloadState>(DownloadState.Idle) val state: StateFlow<DownloadState> = _state.asStateFlow() private var downloadJob: Job? = null fun startDownload(url: String) { downloadJob?.cancel() // Cancel any existing download downloadJob = viewModelScope.launch { // Lifecycle-bound try { downloadAndProcess(url) .collect { state -> _state.value = state } } catch (e: CancellationException) { _state.value = DownloadState.Idle throw e // Re-throw to cooperate with structured concurrency } catch (e: Exception) { _state.value = DownloadState.Error(e.message ?: "Download failed") } } } fun cancel() { downloadJob?.cancel() } private fun downloadAndProcess(url: String): Flow<DownloadState> = flow { // Download phase — IO dispatcher for network val chunks = withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { val connection = URL(url).openConnection() val totalBytes = connection.contentLengthLong val inputStream = connection.getInputStream() val chunks = mutableListOf<ByteArray>() val buffer = ByteArray(8192) var bytesRead = 0L while (true) { ensureActive() // Cooperative cancellation check val read = inputStream.read(buffer) if (read == -1) break chunks.add(buffer.copyOf(read)) bytesRead += read // Emit progress from IO context — Flow handles thread switching } chunks } // Emit download progress chunks.forEachIndexed { index, chunk -> ensureActive() // Check cancellation between chunks emit(DownloadState.Downloading( (index + 1).toFloat() / chunks.size )) } // Process phase — Default dispatcher for CPU work withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { chunks.forEachIndexed { index, chunk -> ensureActive() // Cancellation between processing processChunk(chunk) // Progress emitted after processing } } emit(DownloadState.Complete) } }

Key Architecture Decisions:

1. viewModelScope — Automatic cancellation when ViewModel is cleared (user leaves screen).
2. Flow for progress — Cold stream that emits progress updates; collector controls the lifecycle.
3. withContext(IO) — Network I/O on the IO dispatcher (up to 64 threads).
4. withContext(Default) — CPU-intensive chunk processing on the Default dispatcher (= CPU cores).
5. ensureActive() — Cooperative cancellation check at each iteration. Without this, a tight loop won't respond to cancellation between suspension points.
6. Separate Job tracking — Allows explicit cancellation via cancel button.

Coroutines vs Java Threads: Deep Comparison

An L6 candidate should understand why coroutines are lightweight, not just that they are.

Memory: ~1MB/thread vs ~few hundred bytes/coroutine

Java Thread (OS thread): ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Stack: ~512KB–1MB (OS-allocated) │ │ Kernel thread descriptor │ │ Register file (saved on context switch) │ │ Signal mask, scheduling state │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ Cost: ~1MB per thread. 1000 threads = ~1GB! Kotlin Coroutine (user-space object): ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Continuation object: ~200–400 bytes │ │ (state label + captured variables) │ │ No OS resources until it runs │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ Cost: ~few hundred bytes. 100,000 coroutines = ~40MB.

Suspension vs Blocking

Thread.sleep(5000) — BLOCKING: ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Thread is HELD by the OS │ │ Stack stays in memory (1MB) │ │ CPU registers saved in kernel │ │ Thread can do NOTHING for 5 seconds │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ delay(5000) — SUSPENDING: ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ State saved to HEAP (label + locals) │ │ Thread RELEASED to pool │ │ Thread runs OTHER coroutines │ │ After 5s: scheduler resumes on any │ │ available thread from the pool │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘

Context Switching: Kernel Mode (Heavy) vs Userspace Swap (Light)

Java Thread SwitchCoroutine "Switch"
MechanismOS kernel schedulerUserspace: save label + locals to heap object
Cost~1–10µs (kernel trap, TLB flush, cache invalidation)~100ns (object field writes, function call)
What's savedAll CPU registers, stack pointer, program counter, FPU stateJust the state label (Int) and captured local variables
Preemptive?Yes — OS can interrupt at any pointNo — cooperative. Must hit a suspension point.

withContext: When Does an Actual Thread Switch Happen?

withContext only causes a real thread switch when you change dispatchers. If you're already on the right dispatcher, it's a no-op (no switch).
viewModelScope.launch(Dispatchers.Main) { // Already on Main withContext(Dispatchers.Main) { // NO thread switch — same dispatcher, just runs inline } withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { // REAL thread switch: Main → IO pool thread val data = api.fetch() } // REAL thread switch back: IO → Main withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { // REAL switch: Main → Default pool thread parseJson(data) } // REAL switch back: Default → Main }

Mutex vs ReentrantLock

kotlinx.coroutines.sync.Mutexjava.util.concurrent.locks.ReentrantLock
WaitingSuspends the coroutine (thread released)Blocks the thread (thread held)
Reentrant?NO — same coroutine locking twice = deadlockYes — same thread can re-enter
Use withCoroutines onlyAny Java/Kotlin threads
Dispatcher-safe?Yes — suspension allows other coroutines to runDangerous with limited dispatchers — can starve threads
Mutex is NOT reentrant. This is a common interview trap. If a coroutine already holds a Mutex and tries to lock it again (e.g., calling a function that also locks), it deadlocks. ReentrantLock allows this because it tracks the owning thread.

Thread Pinning: newSingleThreadContext

Some work requires thread affinity — always running on the same thread. Examples: audio processing, OpenGL rendering, or native libraries that use thread-local storage.

// Dispatchers.Default can migrate coroutines between threads withContext(Dispatchers.Default) { // May run on thread-1, suspend, resume on thread-3! processAudio() // BAD if audio lib uses thread-locals } // newSingleThreadContext pins to ONE dedicated thread val audioDispatcher = newSingleThreadContext("AudioThread") withContext(audioDispatcher) { // ALWAYS runs on "AudioThread" — guaranteed affinity processAudio() // Safe for thread-local storage } // Don't forget: audioDispatcher.close() when done (it owns a thread)

What the Compiler Generates: State Machine

The Kotlin compiler transforms every suspend fun into a state machine. Each suspension point becomes a label, and local variables are saved to fields on the Continuation object.

// What you write: suspend fun loadData(): String { val user = fetchUser() // suspension point 1 val posts = fetchPosts(user) // suspension point 2 return "$user: $posts" } // What the compiler generates (simplified): fun loadData(continuation: Continuation<String>): Any? { // The continuation IS the state machine val sm = continuation as? LoadDataContinuation ?: LoadDataContinuation(continuation) when (sm.label) { 0 -> { sm.label = 1 // Next state after resuming val result = fetchUser(sm) // Pass self as callback if (result == COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED // If fetchUser completed synchronously, fall through } 1 -> { sm.user = sm.result as User // Restore result from callback sm.label = 2 val result = fetchPosts(sm.user, sm) if (result == COROUTINE_SUSPENDED) return COROUTINE_SUSPENDED } 2 -> { sm.posts = sm.result as List<Post> return "${sm.user}: ${sm.posts}" // Final result } } } // The Continuation class stores state between suspensions: class LoadDataContinuation( val completion: Continuation<String> ) : Continuation<Any?> { var label = 0 // Current state in the state machine var result: Any? = null var user: User? = null // Local variables become fields var posts: List<Post>? = null override val context: CoroutineContext = completion.context override fun resumeWith(result: Result<Any?>) { this.result = result.getOrThrow() loadData(this) // Re-enter the state machine } }
Key takeaway: There's no magic. The compiler converts sequential-looking suspend code into a callback-based state machine. The Continuation interface is just a callback with context. Each suspend call passes the continuation as its last parameter (CPS — Continuation Passing Style). The "lightweight" nature comes from this: no OS thread needed during suspension, just a heap object holding a label and a few fields.

questions.title: Fix the Buggy Chat ViewModel
questions.description: This ViewModel and Fragment code for a chat streaming feature has 5 bugs. Find and fix ALL of them.

checks.message: Replace GlobalScope with viewModelScope for lifecycle-bound coroutines
Fix the threading issue — LiveData.value must be set on Main thread, or use StateFlow/postValue
Handle cancellation of previous stream when a new one starts
Collection should be lifecycle-aware using repeatOnLifecycle
Guard against re-triggering the API call on configuration change

questions.explanation: Five bugs fixed: (1) GlobalScope replaced with viewModelScope — prevents coroutine leaks. (2) LiveData.value from background thread replaced with StateFlow.update (thread-safe). (3) Previous Job cancelled before starting new stream. (4) Collection wrapped in repeatOnLifecycle to stop collecting when UI not visible. (5) savedInstanceState null check prevents re-triggering on rotation.
hints: Look at the scope — what happens when ViewModel is cleared?
LiveData.value has a threading requirement
What if the user calls streamResponse twice quickly?
lifecycleScope.launch collects even when the app is in background
onViewCreated is called again on rotation — is the API call idempotent?

questions.title: Implement Retry with Exponential Backoff
questions.description: Implement a retryWithBackoff suspend function that retries up to N times with exponential delay (1s, 2s, 4s...), reports attempt progress via a callback, and properly handles CancellationException (never catches it). delay() should be the suspension point that makes cancellation work.

checks.message: Use a loop for retry attempts
Use delay() for cancellation-aware waiting between retries
Explicitly handle CancellationException by re-throwing it
Implement exponential backoff by doubling the delay

questions.explanation: The key insight is CancellationException handling. Coroutine cancellation works by throwing CancellationException at suspension points (like delay()). If you catch Exception broadly, you'll accidentally catch CancellationException (it extends Exception in Kotlin), turning your coroutine into an unkillable zombie. Always re-throw it. The final block() call runs without try/catch so the caller sees the real error.
hints: CancellationException extends Exception in Kotlin — a broad catch will swallow it
The last attempt should NOT be wrapped in try/catch
delay() is the cancellation checkpoint — the coroutine can be cancelled during the delay

questions.title: Configuration Change Survival
questions.description: Analyze each variable. What survives a configuration change (rotation)? What survives process death? What is lost?
options: All survive rotation. Only C and E survive process death.
A, B, C, F survive rotation. D is LOST. C and E survive process death.
A and B survive rotation. Everything else is lost.
Everything survives both rotation and process death.
questions.explanation:
A (draftText): ViewModel field — survives rotation, lost on process death.
B (notes): ViewModel StateFlow — survives rotation, lost on process death.
C (savedId): SavedStateHandle — survives BOTH rotation and process death (backed by Bundle).
D (localDraft): remember {} — LOST on rotation (Composition destroyed and recreated).
E (savedDraft): rememberSaveable {} — survives BOTH rotation and process death (backed by Bundle).
F (notes via collectAsState): Backed by ViewModel StateFlow — survives rotation (resubscribes to same StateFlow), lost on process death.
hints: remember {} vs rememberSaveable {} — one uses the saved instance state Bundle, one does not
ViewModel survives configuration changes but NOT process death
SavedStateHandle is backed by the same Bundle mechanism as onSaveInstanceState

questions.title: Chunked File Download with Progress
questions.description: Design the coroutine architecture for a chunked file download: download a large file, process in chunks, show progress via StateFlow, and allow cancellation. Use withContext(IO) for download, Flow for progress, ensureActive() for cooperative cancellation, and viewModelScope for lifecycle.

checks.message: Use ensureActive() or isActive for cooperative cancellation checks
Support cancellation of the download job

questions.explanation: Key architecture: viewModelScope ties the download to the ViewModel lifecycle. withContext(IO) moves the blocking network read to the IO thread pool. ensureActive() checks for cancellation between chunk reads — without it, a tight read loop won't respond to cancel(). The downloadJob reference allows explicit cancellation. StateFlow provides thread-safe progress updates to the UI.
hints: Track the Job so you can cancel it explicitly
ensureActive() throws CancellationException if the coroutine was cancelled
MutableStateFlow.value is thread-safe — can be set from any dispatcher
Re-throw CancellationException after cleanup

questions.title: Coroutine Memory vs Thread Memory
questions.description: You need 10,000 concurrent tasks that each wait for a network response. What's the approximate memory difference between using Java threads vs Kotlin coroutines?
options: Threads: ~10GB, Coroutines: ~4MB — coroutines save state to heap instead of allocating OS stacks
About the same — coroutines still use threads underneath
Threads: ~100MB, Coroutines: ~100MB — the network buffer dominates
Threads: ~10MB, Coroutines: ~1MB — minor difference
questions.explanation: Each Java thread allocates a ~1MB OS stack, so 10,000 threads = ~10GB of stack memory alone (plus kernel thread descriptors). Each coroutine is a heap object of ~200-400 bytes (the Continuation holding a state label and captured locals), so 10,000 coroutines = ~4MB. The coroutines share a small pool of actual threads (e.g., 64 for Dispatchers.IO). When a coroutine suspends, its state is saved to the heap object and the thread is released to run other coroutines.
hints: Each OS thread needs its own stack — typically 512KB to 1MB
A suspended coroutine is just a small heap object — no thread held

questions.title: Mutex vs ReentrantLock
options: Works fine — Mutex is reentrant like ReentrantLock
DEADLOCK — Mutex is NOT reentrant; the same coroutine trying to lock twice will block forever
Throws IllegalStateException immediately
The inner lock is skipped silently
questions.explanation: Kotlin's Mutex is NOT reentrant. When outerOperation holds the lock and calls innerOperation, the inner mutex.withLock tries to acquire the same Mutex. Since it's already locked, the coroutine suspends waiting for it — but it's the same coroutine that holds the lock, creating a deadlock. Java's ReentrantLock tracks the owning thread and allows re-entry. This is a common interview gotcha.
hints: Mutex suspends the coroutine when it can't acquire the lock
Think about what 'reentrant' means — can the same owner enter twice?

questions.title: The Compiled State Machine
questions.description: The Kotlin compiler transforms this suspend function into a state machine. What does the generated code look like conceptually?
options: It creates a new thread for each suspension point and joins them at the end

It generates a when(label) state machine: label 0 calls fetchUser and saves state, label 1 resumes with the result and calls fetchAvatar, label 2 resumes and returns the Profile. The Continuation object stores the label and local variables between suspensions.
It wraps each call in a CompletableFuture and chains them with thenApply
It inlines the suspend calls and runs them synchronously on the calling thread
questions.explanation: The compiler transforms the function into a state machine with a when(label) switch. Each suspension point becomes a label (0, 1, 2). The function receives a Continuation parameter (the callback). On first call (label=0), it calls fetchUser passing itself as the continuation. When fetchUser completes, it calls continuation.resumeWith(result), which re-enters the function at label=1 with the user result. Local variables (user, avatar) are saved as fields on the Continuation object between suspensions. This is CPS (Continuation Passing Style) — no threads, just callbacks disguised as sequential code.
hints: Each suspension point needs a 'save point' to resume from later
The Continuation interface has resumeWith(result) — it's literally a callback
Local variables can't live on the stack between suspensions — where do they go?

questions.title: withContext Thread Switching
questions.description: Explain when withContext actually causes a real thread switch vs when it's essentially a no-op. Why does this matter for performance?
questions.answer: withContext only causes a real thread switch when the target dispatcher is different from the current one. If you're already on Dispatchers.Main and call withContext(Dispatchers.Main), no thread switch happens — the block runs inline. A real switch happens when changing dispatchers (e.g., Main to IO). This matters for performance because unnecessary withContext calls with the same dispatcher add overhead from context checking without any benefit. Also, Dispatchers.IO and Dispatchers.Default share threads — switching between them may not always involve a real thread switch either, just a dispatcher context change.
questions.explanation: Understanding this prevents unnecessary withContext wrapping. A common mistake is wrapping every suspend call in withContext(IO) "just to be safe" — if the suspend function already handles its own dispatching (as most Retrofit/Room calls do), the extra withContext is wasted overhead. Conversely, if you're calling a blocking Java API from Main, you MUST use withContext(IO) because the API doesn't suspend — it blocks.
hints: What happens if you call withContext with the same dispatcher you're already on?
Do Dispatchers.IO and Dispatchers.Default share any threads?

questions.title: Thread Pinning for Audio Processing
questions.description: You're processing audio buffers that use thread-local storage for state. Which dispatcher should you use?
options: Dispatchers.Default — it's CPU-bound work
Dispatchers.IO — it's I/O-like processing
Dispatchers.Main — to avoid threading issues
newSingleThreadContext("AudioThread") — guarantees thread affinity for thread-local storage
questions.explanation: Dispatchers.Default and Dispatchers.IO can migrate coroutines between threads. After a suspension point, the coroutine may resume on a different thread, which means ThreadLocal state would be lost or wrong. newSingleThreadContext("AudioThread") creates a dedicated single thread — the coroutine ALWAYS runs on that specific thread, preserving thread-local storage. This is essential for native libraries, audio processing, or OpenGL contexts that have thread affinity requirements. Remember to call .close() on the dispatcher when done, as it owns the thread.
hints: ThreadLocal storage is per-thread — what happens if the coroutine migrates?
Default and IO dispatchers use thread pools — no guarantee of same thread

questions.title: Lifecycle-Aware Flow Collection
questions.description: This Compose screen collects a Flow but wastes resources when the app is in the background. The Flow is a location stream that fires every second. Fix it to only collect when the lifecycle is at least STARTED.

checks.message: Use collectAsStateWithLifecycle() which is lifecycle-aware (stops collecting in background)

questions.explanation: collectAsState() collects the Flow regardless of lifecycle state — even when the app is in the background. For a location stream firing every second, this wastes battery and CPU. collectAsStateWithLifecycle() (from androidx.lifecycle:lifecycle-runtime-compose) automatically stops collection when the lifecycle falls below STARTED (app goes to background) and restarts when it comes back. It's the Compose equivalent of repeatOnLifecycle(Lifecycle.State.STARTED).
hints: collectAsState() doesn't know about the Android lifecycle
There's a lifecycle-aware variant designed specifically for this
